spans.content: Performance and cost of materials for lithium-based rechargeable automotive batteries
Richard Schmuch<sup>1</sup>, Ralf Wagner<sup>1</sup>, Gerhard Hörpel<sup>2</sup>, Tobias Placke<sup>1*</sup> and Martin Winter<sup>1,3*</sup>
It is widely accepted that for electric vehicles to be accepted by consumers and to achieve wide market penetration, ranges of at least

at an affordable cost are required. Therefore, significant improvements to lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) in terms of energy density and cost along the battery value chain are required, while other key performance indicators, such as lifetime, safety, fast-charging ability and low-temperature performance, need to be enhanced or at least sustained. Here, we review advances and challenges in LIB materials for automotive applications, in particular with respect to cost and performance parameters. The production processes of anode and cathode materials are discussed, focusing on material abundance and cost. Advantages and challenges of different types of electrolyte for automotive batteries are examined. Finally, energy densities and costs of promising battery chemistries are critically evaluated along with an assessment of the potential to fulfil the ambitious targets of electric vehicle propulsion.
In retrospect, the years from 1900 to 1912 are remembered as the golden era of electric vehicles (EVs)
. In 1912, the number of EVs in use amounted to 30,000 in the United States
. Most of these EVs were powered by a lead-acid battery (LAB), which had been invented by Sinsteden (1854) and Planté (1859); each cell delivered a cell voltage of around
). Due to the poor active mass utilization and unfavourable charge-discharge mechanism in LABs
, their practical energy content only amounts to 40 Wh
. Their Coulombic efficiency and energy efficiency are also only moderate and sum up to

. Today, nearly all of the 1.3 billion vehicles on the road worldwide use LABs for starting, lighting and ignition (SLI) applications. After a century of combustion engine supremacy, the imperative to decarbonize the transport sector is expected to lead to another golden era of electric mobility: in 2016, 160,000 plug-in hybrid electric vehicles (PHEVs) were sold in the US alone
Nickel-metal hydride (NiMH) batteries have been the primary choice for hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs). NiMH cells have a nominal cell voltage of
and can deliver an energy content of up to
. Nowadays, PHEVs and battery-electric vehicles (BEVs) solely use lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) for propulsion.
Next to their higher energy contents of up to
at cell level, as well as higher Coulombic
, the main advantage of LIBs lies in the possibility of designing a vast range of different power to energy ratios (P:E), such as P:E = 15, 8 or 3 for HEVs, PHEVs or BEVs, respectively
. To achieve mass market penetration, the US Department of Energy and the Advanced Battery Consortium estimated that a driving range of at least
is required for BEVs, which corresponds to approximately
. Further, the pack cost needs to fall below
). It should be noted that there are other more optimistic views on energy and cost. State-of-the-art automotive LIB packs show up to

To meet energy and cost targets, improvements along the whole battery value chain are needed (Fig. 1a). Besides LIBs, lithium metal-based batteries with solid electrolytes are also considered for EV application. The cell design concepts of LIB cells with two insertion electrodes and of solid-state lithium metal-based cells are depicted in Fig. 1b. This Review surveys the state-of-the-art and recent advances in active materials, electrolytes, separators and cell chemistries for automotive batteries. We focus on three most important aspects: performance, production and cost. We also give a perspective on future developments at the end.

With respect to anode chemistries, carbonaceous materials, in particular synthetic and artificial graphites (SGs) and natural graphites (NGs) as well as amorphous (hard and soft) carbons, can be considered as state-of-the-art negative electrode materials
. With respect to EV applications, and compared to NGs, SGs show outstandingly high levels of purity and less fluctuating quality
. Quite often, mixtures of amorphous and graphitic carbons are used to optimize the different characteristics with respect to application, for example, to optimize the P:E ratio
. Currently, in some commercial cells (for example, Panasonic or Hitachi Maxell-Kopin), small amounts of silicon (mostly as
) are added to the carbon anode to further enhance cell energy
. Also, lithium titanate (LTO) is utilized in commercial cells (for example, Toshiba's SCiB)

. However, due to their low resulting cell voltage and high-power capability, LTO-based cells are more suitable for power applications, particularly for recuperation such as in electric buses; thus, they are not further discussed in this work. Lithium metal is considered as the most promising future anode material, in particular for application in all-solid-state batteries (ASSBs) using ceramic or polymeric electrolytes; currently it is already practically used in Li-metal polymer batteries by Bollore
for amorphous carbon, clearly evidences the dominance of carbon-based anode materials. In contrast, LTO and Si-based anode materials only make up around

Fig. 1 | Battery value chain and lithium ion versus Li-metal cell chemistries. a, Value chain of rechargeable batteries for electric mobility. b, Schematic cross-sections of a lithium-ion battery (LIB, left) and an all-solid-state battery (ASSB) cell configuration (right). Thickness data refer to state-of-the-art lithium-ion batteries and to suitable ASSBs (while even lower values for the solid electrolyte and Li-metal anode are targeted).
Figure 2 displays the production processes for state-of-the-art graphite-based anode materials, for Li-metal foil as well as possible commercial routes for production of Si-C and

-Si-C composites. It is difficult to get detailed information from material manufacturers for the latter processes. The processing of SG (Fig. 2a) comprises of three major steps. First, the precursor material, for example, a type of coke, is transferred into an intermediate material, the so-called soft (graphitizable) carbon, by calcination at
. The soft carbon is further treated by crushing and grinding, and the particles are classified according to their particle size (range in
). The material is graphitized in a second step at temperatures higher than

for several days to weeks including cooling phases. In the final step, typically mechanical particle refinement is conducted by grinding, classifying and eventually coating to optimize particle design[13-17]. The production of NG (Fig. 2b) starts with the mining from graphite ores and several steps for separation and concentration of the flaky graphite particles, particularly by flotation[15,16,18,19]. Thereby, purity levels of around

can be reached, with the main impurities being silicate mineral grains and chemical compounds of S, K, Na, and so on. Then, graphite flakes are treated by sophisticated milling techniques to obtain spherical particles in the

range (via micronization and spheroidization)[14,15,19]. The mechanical treatment is followed by wet-chemical and/or thermal purification processes, most likely leaching with hydrofluoric acid or thermal treatment at over
to decrease the impurity content to less than
. Similar to SG processing, the final

step focuses on particle refinement. The carbon coating process is a highly important step to ensure good performance\(^{16}\). The price for NG amounts to around 8 US\( kg\)^{-1}\), while as of 2016 SG had a higher price of around 13 US\) kg\(^{-1}\). This is mostly related to the energy cost during graphitization\(^{12}\). NG has been categorized as a critical, strategic material by several governments including in the US and Europe; carbons and graphites can, however, always be synthesized. In particular, the price for SG is expected to decrease significantly (to less than 10 US\) kg\(^{-1}\) in 2025), which is related to the increasing demand and possible supply bottlenecks for NG.
Even though various reports for lab-scale preparation of Si-C and
composite anode materials have been published, there is not much information about commercial production processes publicly available. It is known that so far only a small mass fraction of Si or

5 wt%) is added to the anode[1]. We present two practically suitable production processes for Si-based anode materials (Fig. 2c). Si nanoparticles are typically prepared by plasma gas phase synthesis, for example, by pyrolysis of silanes[20], while
particles are obtained by reduction of SiO or

precursors, which can be performed by milling or gas phase reactions[21,22]. In the following step, the Si-based active materials have to be embedded in a carbon matrix, for example by mixing with graphite or carbon precursors followed by additional carbonization and milling procedures. In the final step, the composite particles can be further optimized, for example by carbon coating to decrease the specific surface area and, thus, reaction with the electrolyte[16].

Fig. 2 | Production processes for negative electrode materials for LIB and Li-metal cells. a-d, Schematic illustration of industrial syntheses of synthetic graphite/soft carbon (a), natural graphite (b), Si-C or

The production of Li-metal can start from several natural raw materials, but most often from brines, which are first concentrated via evaporation and converted to LiCl before purification (Fig. 2d). Highly purified molten anhydrous LiCl is processed via electrolysis to Li metal[23]. Distillation results in the removal of low-melting-point impurities such as Na. In the next step, Li-metal is shaped, for example, into foil by extrusion and/or rolling, with purity and thickness being the most important characteristics[17,24,25]. Finally, Li-metal foil is typically stabilized by surface passivation, for example, by gas treatment, by coating with a layer of polyethylene wax or by surface fluorination[26]. Recent price fluctuations for metallic lithium has led to cost concerns for Li-metal batteries such as
. Considering a Li-metal ingot price of 50-130 US
(based on Li in the raw material
to Li-metal conversion factor of 5.3, which accounts for the molar lithium content within
[28,29], and costs for processing to thin foils, one may easily end up with a Li-foil price of 300-400 US
. However, it has also been communicated that processing to thin foils (much thinner than 100
m) can even lead to costs of over 1,000 US
depending on the processing technique (evaporation, vapour deposition, extrusion and/or rolling) and the targeted Li-foil thickness. These costs are higher than LIB materials (much less than 100 US
) and Li-metal based chemistries may have an uncertain future, unless the costs of Li-metal and its
processing decrease significantly in the future and inexpensive shaping technologies for the Li-metal electrode are developed

Today, a trend to gradually increase the silicon content in graphite or carbon-based anodes is observed, while there might be a sharper future technology change going from LIBs to Li-metal-based ASSBs. The performance characteristics of anode materials, including specific and volumetric capacities as well as energy efficiencies, are summarized in Fig. 3a. The challenges of Si-based anode materials, such as volume change upon de-/lithiation and related mechanical degradation and active lithium loss
, as well as safety issues of lithium metal anodes
have been reported widely. Furthermore, various concepts to enhance the mechanical stability of Si-based composite anodes
and to mitigate active lithium loss (for example, by pre-lithiation)
in LIBs as well as to stabilize the Li-metal surface for ASSBs have been presented

Since the commercialization of LIBs, the cathode has proven a bottleneck with regard to specific capacities. Key requirements for positive active materials for automotive batteries include high specific and volumetric capacities and high discharge potentials versus
, high intrinsic safety, high tap density, fast kinetics and good capacity retention. Due to their high technological maturity,
-type layered oxide cathodes, which incorporate transition metals (M) such as nickel, cobalt and manganese (NMC) or nickel,
© 2018 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

The energy content was calculated using a spreadsheet-based calculation tool, using material densities and electrochemical properties (such as practical discharge potential and redox potential) as input parameters. The calculations assume a NMC positive electrode with a redox potential of
(a) and a graphitic negative electrode with a redox potential of

(b). The energy efficiency (defined as discharge energy divided by charge energy<sup>7</sup>) values refer to electrodes with typical electrode compositions and active material particles sizes of commercial batteries at a current rate of 1C.
cobalt and aluminium (NCA), are currently most widely used as positive active materials for automotive batteries
In contrast to the electrochemically inactive
ions and the one-electron process of the
redox couple, a part of the nickel (depending on the Mn content) offers a two-electron redox process

in NMC-type oxides. In general, a higher Ni content implies a higher capacity. A widely adapted strategy to get the most out of the energy content of NMC positives therefore lies in maximizing the Ni content (Fig. 3b)
. Under this strategy, the specific capacity can be increased from around
at an almost unchanged redox potential. Ni-rich layered cathodes such as NMC-811 or NCA
, however, currently have a significantly shorter cycle life and a lower thermal stability and so are less safe
. Nevertheless, layered oxide cathodes with Ni contents exceeding
. Comparing NCA and NMC-811, which provide very similar practical capacities, NCA shows advantages with regard to

capacity retention, a smaller tendency for transition metal dissolution (no Mn), and power, while NMC-811 shows more favourable properties with regard to thermal stability (more inactive Mn). Ni-rich cathodes exhibit a higher reactivity towards moisture and the battery electrolyte, which may require additional measures during synthesis, electrode processing and cell operation. Besides the optimization of the elemental composition, material coating and the choice of adequate electrolyte formulations are key considerations for these cathode materials[41].

A promising approach to increase the energy density of layered oxides, while preserving cycle life and safety, has been established[41], involving the synthesis of spherical NMC particles with a Ni-rich core and Mn-rich shell. While the particle core provides high capacity, the Mn-enriched shell exhibits an enhanced stability versus the electrolyte. Since its initial report in
, this concept has been continuously optimized with full concentration-gradient NMC (FCG-NMC) particles with Ni contents greater than
(ref. [44]). Despite its high average nickel content, the capacity retention of FCG-NMC is claimed to exceed that of
Regardless of these promising developments, the maximum theoretical energy contents of around
at material level could mark the ultimate physicochemical limit of insertion cathode materials involving a single electron transfer per transition metal[45,46].
Li-rich and Mn-rich oxides (LMR-NMC) constitute a structurally related materials class that provides exceptionally high specific capacities of up to

at a slightly lower discharge potential than conventional NMC. A higher Mn content of LMR-NMC cathodes results in a significant cost advantage over conventional NMC, but also leads to the conversion from the layered to spinel structure upon cycling. This conversion causes voltage and capacity fade, which are most critical performance issues. Further downsides of LMR-NMC lie in its lower crystallographic density, slow kinetics, and the voltage hysteresis caused by the anionic redox activity[48,50].
Figure 4 illustrates the synthesis processes of transition metal oxides
as well as of lithium transition metal phosphates
. Layered oxides with mixed transition metal composition are typically prepared over a co-precipitation route. An aqueous solution of the transition metal salts (for example,

) are pumped into a continuously stirred tank reactor (CSTR), in which the metal hydroxide or carbonate precipitates. After filtering and repeatedly washing to remove impurities, the material is dried and sieved before it is mixed with a stoichiometric amount of a lithium salt. While
is significantly cheaper, Ni-rich cathodes, such as NMC-811, have more favourable physical and electrochemical properties when calcined with LiOH (ref.
). The desired product is formed through calcination at
. The as-prepared cathode powder is then deagglomerated and may optionally be surface treated to stabilize the electrode/electrolyte interface and, thus, electrochemical performance

Lithium metal phosphates such as LFP are accessible through numerous synthesis routes. Industrial production, however, often takes a two-step route: the first step involves the preparation of the precursor, which may be prepared through spray-drying of an aqueous solution, already containing all required reactants[55]. The implementation of carbon is crucial to overcome the intrinsically low electronic conductivity of this materials class[56]. The desired product is finally obtained through calcination within an inert or mildly reducing atmosphere.
The cathode active material accounts for more than
of the cost of current NMC-based LIBs, while raw material makes up more than
of the cathode cost[51]. The world's cobalt reserves are geographically highly concentrated in politically instable regions in
© 2018 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
Fig. 4 | Production processes for positive electrode materials for LIB cells. a,b, Schematic illustration of industrial syntheses of lithium transition metal oxides: layered oxides

Central Africa, allegedly involving child labour and severe environmental damage. The cobalt price has undergone strong fluctuations within the last decade and has recently risen markedly[57]. The chosen transition metal composition of the cathode therefore not only has a large impact on energy density, cycle life and safety, but also on the battery cost[58]. Due to the high cobalt price, cobalt-free positives such as the high-voltage spinel oxide

and Co-free LMR-oxides are being discussed as potential low-cost, environmentally benign alternative positives[45]. Despite its low energy content (see Fig. 3b) and its limited cycle life (particularly at elevated temperatures), the cubic spinel oxide

(LMO) is also still used in many of today's EV batteries. LMO has an excellent rate capability at low cost and is, thus, often added in significant amounts to cathode material blends (see Table 1)[8].

Global lithium reserves may be considered less critical, although lithium brines are geographically concentrated in South America and China[59]. Lithium prices have been continuously on the rise in past years, mainly due to the fast-growing demand of the battery market, which currently consumes around

of worldwide production[60]. It should be noted that the commonly quoted 'lithium price' often refers to lithium carbonate. Accordingly, the price of refined pure Li metal is significantly higher. In view of the expected further growth of the battery market, an increase of the currently negligible recycling quota for lithium should be strived for to make electric mobility more sustainable. Global nickel reserves are geographically less concentrated and sufficiently large to sustain future cathode material production. Today, the majority of nickel consumption goes to steel production and only a minor share falls into battery materials. Despite its high economic importance, no supply risk is presently to be expected for nickel[61]. Reserves of further battery-relevant transition metals such as iron, aluminium and manganese are non-critical, so these non-toxic elements are available at very low cost.
Commercial LIBs typically contain electrolytes (still almost exclusively) based on lithium hexafluorophosphate
as conducting salt that is dissolved in mixtures of cyclic and linear organic carbonate solvents[62,63]. The salt concentration is typically in the range of
in order to achieve maximum ionic conductivity

. To tailor the performance of LIBs with regard to a desired functionality, electrolyte additives are added in small concentrations of less than 5 wt% to leave the bulk electrolyte properties unchanged
. The most commonly used cyclic carbonate is ethylene carbonate (EC)
. EC is produced commercially via the catalytic oxidation of ethylene with oxygen to yield ethylene oxide, which is then reacted with CO
in the presence of an anion exchange resin catalyst (Fig. 5a). The production of the linear carbonate dimethyl carbonate (DMC) is based on oxidative carbonylation of methanol
. DMC is reacted with ethanol in a transesterification to form diethyl carbonate (DEC)
. The two symmetrical linear organic carbonates DMC and DEC are reacted in the presence of a catalyst to form ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC) in a disproportionation reaction

. Organic carbonates are high-volume commodity chemicals that are derived by a C1 or C2 route (Fig. 5a) from cracker reactors. Beyond their relatively small-volume use in (electro-)chemical applications, they are applied as solvents in industrial cleaners, cosmetics, paint strippers, and lubricants, as well as plasticizers and intermediates for production of polycarbonates. In contrast, lithium salts are fine chemicals, only used in electrochemical (and chemical) applications. The lithium salt, mainly LiPF
, is the main cost driver in carbonate-based electrolytes. LiPF
is produced by reacting lithium fluoride with phosphorous pentachloride in anhydrous hydrogen fluoride (AHF). Thereby, PF
is further purified with additional AHF (Fig. 5b)

in various purity classes is achieved, starting from 99.0% for industrial grade to 99.99% for EV plus grade. Depending on the purity and composition, prices for carbonate-based electrolytes range from 7–20 US$ kg
Recent studies verified, contrary to common belief in literature, that these state-of-the-art electrolytes are oxidatively quite stable up to
, and thus can be used in combination with high voltage cathode materials[73]. The main challenge of these electrolytes is their poor intrinsic safety, especially at elevated temperatures
. Linear carbonates are highly volatile and flammable, for example, DMC has a flash point of only
is highly susceptible towards hydrolysis and decomposes under formation of highly toxic organophosphates and highly corrosive hydrogen fluoride[75].

The use of a liquid organic electrolyte within the LIB inevitably requires a porous membrane as separator, placed between the negative and positive electrode to prevent electrical short circuits
. The annual separator demand in LIB cells added up to roughly

). Technically, four basic separator types are currently available, differing in product chemistry and production process: (1) microporous membranes, (2) non-woven mats, (3) ceramic-coated separators and (4) solid inorganic, polymeric or hybrid electrolytes, where the latter combine the electrolyte and separator function in one
. The majority of the microporous separators are based on polyolefins (PO: polypropylene, polyethylene)

. Single-layer separators are either produced in a dry process (Fig. 5c), starting from melt extrusion, annealing and stretching (mono- or bi-axial), or in a wet process, starting from a polymer solution, extrusion, solvent extraction and stretching, at production cost often below
). Nonwoven mats are the separator of choice for lead-acid batteries
, but are frequently used in LIB-related research. However, traditional manufacturing processes such as melt blowing result in large pore size, inappropriate for practical LIBs
. Therefore, non-woven separators for use in LIBs are produced by electrospinning or additional coating
. However, production costs exceed the PO separator costs

, carbon dioxide; EtOH, ethanol; DMC, dimethyl carbonate; DEC, diethyl carbonate; EMC, ethyl methyl carbonate; EC, ethylene carbonate; C1, one-carbon synthesis route; C2, two-carbon synthesis route; AHF, anhydrous hydrogen fluoride.
by a factor of two or more, currently limiting non-woven separators in LIBs to niche markets
. Microporous membranes and nonwoven mats may be additionally coated with ceramic
. Ceramic coating supports higher temperature tolerance and, thus, safety, which is increasingly demanded for large battery cells for the car industry
. Further, ceramic particles also improve the wettability of the separator
. Despite their multilayer architecture, the thickness of separators is
or less, enabling high volumetric energy and power density
. It is common understanding that those separators containing (basic) ceramic particles can absorb HF generated during cycling and, thus, may support longer cycle life.
To replace unsafe organic solvents in the conventional liquid-electrolyte-separator set-up, so-called solid electrolytes, serving as both separator and electrolyte, are attracting increasing interest
. Solid electrolytes can be classified into two major categories: solid polymer electrolytes (SPEs) and inorganic solid electrolytes either having a crystalline, glass or glass-ceramic structure
. It is believed that solid electrolytes will enable rechargeable Li-metal anodes, which can boost the energy density at cell level

. SPEs based on poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO) together with Li-metal anode and LFP cathode are already used as traction cells in car-sharing programs. Due to the poor room-temperature conductivity of SPEs, the battery packs are operated at
, which reduces the usable electric energy at pack level
. Further, PEO has a low oxidative stability, limiting the usable cathode chemistry to materials operating below

Contrary to common belief in the research community, some inorganic solid electrolytes have even higher room-temperature conductivities than conventional liquid electrolytes, but often have smaller electrochemical stability windows[40]. The major challenge is to conform the solid active material/solid electrolyte interface and, thus, maintain the proceeding interfacial reactions during cycling, in particular as shape-change and insertion electrode materials continuously swell and shrink during operation[40]. Until recently, inorganic solid electrolytes were only commercially used in thin-film batteries based on lithium phosphorous oxynitride (LIPON)[81]. Still, their high cost hampers a wider market penetration of these thin-film cells[40]. Considering the already very low cost liquid electrolyte and separator as a benchmark, there is not much room for reducing the material processing cost of solid electrolytes[40]. A cost disadvantage certainly lies in the typically high lithium content in the solid electrolytes discussed, whose development is still at an early stage. The future role of Li-metal-based ASSBs and the potential displacement of LIBs greatly depends on major technological breakthroughs.

; LMR-NMC, lithium- and manganese-rich NMC; TSE, thiophosphate-based solid electrolyte (for example,

have therefore become attainable. As a result of enhanced ranges, car manufacturers are willing to compromise on the targeted cycle number (1,300-2,000) (ref. 8), as the electricity throughput of a battery (around 40 MWh) to drive an EV for a certain accumulated tyre mileage (for example,
) is more critical than the cycle number. However, the cycle number, and not the total electricity throughput, is often seen as critical parameter, which is still a popular misconception.

Since its market introduction, the energy content of LIBs has increased remarkably as a result of numerous material and engineering measures. Prismatic, cylindrical or pouch cell designs are used in automotive applications, even though the highest specific energy and energy density at cell level is currently achieved by small cylindrical 18650-type cells (around

) (refs 6,40). The LIB technology does not rely on specialized cell chemistries, so various material combinations can be applied to meet desired requirements. This becomes clear when looking at the material combinations used in commercial cells (Table 1). The majority of EV cells are based on synthetic graphite anodes, while recently, small amounts of silicon were added to the anode for some cells. If silicon can be successfully established in the anode, without shortening cycle life excessively, further energy density improvements could be realized.

Various cathode active materials or combinations are commercially applied, in particular Ni-rich layered cathodes, which are commonly blended with LMO for high power density and safety. With respect to further energy density improvements, a further decrease of the LMO content is, therefore, an obvious strategy. Moreover, the Ni content in layered cathodes has continuously increased to enhance capacity. Currently, NCA, NMC-532 and NMC-622 can be considered as state-of-the-art cathode materials. Due to its

relatively low volumetric capacity, lithium iron phosphate (LFP), which is used in passenger cars in China at a large scale, is a candidate for replacement by higher capacity layered oxides. Due to the robustness of LFP, there remains an opportunity in heavy-duty applications, such as buses and trucks, where the material's long cycle life and good rate capability come into play.

Figure 6 displays the electrode stack level energy content of various cell chemistries, including state-of-the-art chemistries and future LIB and Li-metal technologies. The ellipses are based on the calculation of different values for positive electrode thicknesses, ranging from
(corresponding to lower energy content) up to

(corresponding to higher energy content). The utilization of thicker and/or denser electrodes is a suitable strategy to increase the energy, if the power demands can still be fulfilled at the same time. Currently, coating thicknesses of around

are used for high energy cells. Further energy improvements can be achieved using high-capacity anode and cathode materials, that is, Si and LMR-NMC or NMC-811, or by moving to the Li-metal technology. As proposed by various researchers and companies, lithium metal anodes will most likely only be realized in conjunction with solid electrolytes, which are used to provide sufficient safety that cannot be accomplished with current liquid electrolytes, although ionic liquid-based electrolytes show some promise[83]. The overall gain in energy for Li-metal batteries will also be strongly influenced by the excess of Li-metal (Fig. 6), which might vary between
, depending on the extent of parasitic, Li-consuming reactions reducing the active lithium content during operation.
ASSBs involving an inorganic solid electrolyte might constitute the first disruptive innovation in the field of rechargeable Li battery technology since its introduction

. In comparison to LIBs, a leap towards higher energy densities could be realized if a Li-metal anode can be safely and reversibly utilized. It is also envisioned that multiple bipolar ASSB stacks can be connected in series without the need for individual cell housings, which would in turn increase the active:inactive ratio in terms of both volume and weight and, thus, increase the overall energy density of

. However, bipolar configuration also means that there is no single cell management control and thus very careful cell capacity balancing is necessary. Other promises of ASSBs include an improved high-power capability, enhanced thermal stability, and extended cycle and calendar life
. Currently, thio-LISICON-type solid electrolytes from the
system and oxidic electrolytes such as NASICON-type
are among the most promising materials classes for advanced
. Toyota have announced that they will introduce ASSB-powered EVs with sulphide-based solid electrolytes by

. However, each solid electrolyte chemistry faces individual scientific and technological challenges, including limited chemical stability towards other cell components, instability towards exposure to atmosphere or moisture, difficulties in processability, and high interfacial resistances. Furthermore, the cycling stability against Li-metal still has to be proven and the solid/solid interfaces have to be tailored carefully to achieve suitable performance. Besides ASSBs, lithium-sulphur (Li-S) batteries were considered as an alternative high-energy battery for automotive applications, mainly due to their exceptionally high theoretical specific energy at the material level and the low cost of sulphur

. These theoretical values often deviate markedly from practical values, as the large weight and volume fraction of inactive battery components such as the electrolyte or conductive additive as well as the volume expansion of the active materials are not taken into account
is considered more critical than specific energy
. Optimized Li-S cells could outperform advanced LIBs in terms of
, and would then not be attractive for EVs, but rather for applications with need for high specific energies (such as aviation, military applications)

batteries have been considered as a potential successor of the LIB, owing to the high theoretical energy density on the material level. On a closer look at the electrode

, which includes the mass and volume of the required inactive and auxiliary components, the projected energy density is significantly reduced. Lithium-air batteries are currently still at a very early stage of development. The many technical challenges are not expected to be overcome within the next decade and it remains to be seen whether this technology will find application in electric vehicles in the far future.
Fast-charging capabilities for automotive batteries have drawn much focus, as this factor is crucial for consumer acceptance. The target of various car manufacturers is to achieve

state-of-charge (SOC) within 5–20 minutes[82]. To reach this goal, the cells and battery pack have to be specifically designed to meet the demanding charging requirements, at the expense of the energy density, for example by using higher amounts of LMO in the cathode or by additional measures for cell cooling. In addition, the risk of increased capacity fading and more severe safety considerations have to be taken into account. For example, to fully charge a
range; Table 1) within 20 minutes (more than 3C rate), a charging power of
would be required, which, in turn, leads to a driving range gain of

per minute of charge. In comparison, an internal combustion engine-driven vehicle (ICEVs) can be refuelled with an equivalent rate of 12C. In contrast, a rate of 0.3–0.5C is typically sufficient for battery discharge.
The cost of EV battery packs has continuously declined in recent years. The price of LIB battery packs has fallen below 300 US
and is expected to decrease further
. To achieve cost competitiveness with combustion-engine-driven vehicles, the pack cost needs to fall below 120-150 US

). Cost savings have been partly realized through up-scaling and automation of the battery production in large industrial plants. The large investments for a large battery factory make market entry for new players very difficult. Furthermore, cost competitiveness with established manufacturers is difficult to achieve. Traditional car manufacturers are currently not likely to commit to their own battery-cell production, while start-up companies, joint ventures or automotive component suppliers are more likely to do so

. Apart from economies of scale, further cost savings may be realized by changing the cell type, for example, by enlarging the dimensions of cylindrical cells from 18650 to 21700 format, so that the active:inactive material volume ratio is increased.

The main cost reduction drivers of EV cells are related to manufacturing and production improvements, such as increasing yield and throughput rate, as well as decreasing material cost per kWh. Furthermore, producing higher volumes of materials will result in lower costs per unit. Nevertheless, there are various risks when targeting such low cost levels, particularly with respect to the raw material cost of cobalt, nickel, lithium and copper
. In October 2017, copper prices rose to their highest level in three years. The copper price is however not critical at cell level, since only small quantities are needed as current collector

. However, with regard to the cell hardware, wiring system components in the EV as well as the infrastructure for fast charging, copper can be regarded as a strategic material, meaning that it is a strategic requirement to recycle copper and to promote eco-designed products with limited copper usage

. Supply risks of LIB raw materials have been evaluated in various studies, pointing out that the highest risk category with respect to material reserves and supply reliability includes cobalt and lithium
. However, concerns about Li shortage are not shared by all experts in the field.

Figure 7 illustrates projected cost per kWh for various cell chemistries, which already have been considered for the energy calculations at stack level (see Fig. 1b). With respect to LIB technologies, it is obvious that for a given volume the cost per kWh decreases with increasing energy content. The estimated cost for Li-metal-based ASSBs, despite having higher energy densities, is significantly higher compared to the high-energy LIB technologies, which is mainly due to the high cost of Li-metal (250-1,000 US
m thickness Li foil). When using a thicker Li-metal foil, the Cu current collector foil can theoretically be omitted, which would result in a marginal cost saving of roughly 2 US
. The high Li-metal costs also make Li-S and

based chemistries more expensive than regularly claimed. Nevertheless, it has to be kept in mind that the cost for lithium metal foil strongly depends on the targeted thickness and processing technique, such as extrusion, rolling or even vapour deposition or evaporation, as indicated by the Li metal price uncertainty in Fig. 7. Due to unavailable data, the cost for the solid electrolyte has been roughly estimated as 50 US
. Furthermore, the commonly claimed safety benefits of ASSBs also have to be verified in large-scale cells at various states of health.
It is widely expected that rechargeable batteries will require energy contents of around 235 Wh
at pack level to achieve a driving range beyond

can be achieved at battery pack level. To achieve these demanding energy targets, several strategies have been followed from material to pack level, such as the implementation of a higher Si-content in high-energy anodes and the increase of the
© 2018 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
Ni content in layered cathode materials. Advances in the attainable driving range have been realized in recent years, as the energy content has grown to

per EV. With increasing driving range per cycle, car manufacturers are in turn willing to compromise on the attainable cycle number, as the overall energy throughput of a battery is more crucial. Energy improvements will only be possible if the power capability is not severely compromised, as the feasibility of fast charging (that is,
) will be critical for user acceptance.
Battery pack cost needs to fall below 125 US
to achieve cost competitiveness with ICEVs. At the same time, ICEVs are likely to become increasingly expensive due to stricter
emission regulations. Currently, the battery costs range from 100-170 US
at the cell and pack level, respectively, which strongly depends on the chosen cell chemistry. It can be assumed that the cost target of 125 US

at pack level will be achieved by 2022-2025, while the cost reduction drivers are economies of scale and a higher degree of automation in production. Cobalt is considered as the most critical raw material with respect to cost, material reserves and supply reliability, while concerns about Li availability are not shared by all experts in the field.

We expect a further improvement of today's LIBs with respect to both energy and cost. Currently, due to a favourable mix of numerous parameters, the LIB is superior to alternative cell technologies, where research is driven by individual (and often theoretical) advantages over current LIBs. It is also possible that new LIB cell chemistries — with even higher capacities than projected on today's material roadmaps — will be developed in the future.

To achieve a comparable mix of advantageous properties with alternative chemistries will certainly take time, if it can ever be realized. Therefore, LIBs are likely to still dominate automotive applications within the coming decade. Nevertheless, it also is clear from today's perspective that there will not be only one universal LIB technology for all applications, but rather a variety of chemistries and configurations for specialized applications. Considering the long development cycles of the automotive industry, it is currently unlikely that LIB technology will be replaced by any 'next generation' technology such as Li-metal based chemistries (ASSBs, Li-S,

) or by technologies beyond lithium (such as Na- or Mg-based chemistries) in the next decade. Considering the large financial investments already committed to the large-scale production of LIBs worldwide, it becomes increasingly more difficult for emerging battery technologies to be cost competitive. From today's perspective, it is only reasonable to state that the next generation of automotive batteries will be a further improved version of current LIBs.
